title: Example Code
code_blocks:
import json

# Energy efficiency assessment framework
def assess_energy_efficiency(system_data):
    """Evaluate energy claims against thermodynamic reality."""
    claimed = system_data.get("claimed_efficiency", 0)
    measured = system_data.get("measured_efficiency", 0)
    waste = system_data.get("waste_heat_ratio", 0)

    # Flag impossible claims (> Carnot limit)
    carnot_limit = 1 - (system_data.get("cold_temp", 300) /
                        system_data.get("hot_temp", 600))

    return {
        "claimed": claimed,
        "measured": measured,
        "carnot_limit": round(carnot_limit, 3),
        "claim_valid": claimed <= carnot_limit,
        "waste_ratio": waste,
        "assessment": "concern" if claimed > carnot_limit else "plausible"
    }

# Example usage
result = assess_energy_efficiency({
    "claimed_efficiency": 0.85,
    "measured_efficiency": 0.42,
    "waste_heat_ratio": 0.58,
    "cold_temp": 300,
    "hot_temp": 600
})
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
formulas: •	Active motors & processors: 6 hours × 2 kW = 12 kWh (~43.2 MJ)
lists: Energy inputs in joules — everything that powers the human or machine (food, electricity, fuel, etc.)
Time/labor hours — the total active involvement required, including rest, maintenance, and supervision
Hidden energy costs — maintenance, replacement, material wear, supply chain, redundancy
Humans are messy: metabolism, cognitive load, fatigue, error rates, and physical environment all affect energy use and productive hours.
Machines are also messy: electricity input, maintenance, downtime, calibration, cooling, failure cascades.
Hidden dependencies dominate: supply chains, logistics, waste handling, and redundancy are part of the cost but rarely counted.
The units are different: human “energy input” comes via calories (≈4.18 kJ per kcal), while machines use electricity or fuel with different conversion efficiencies.
Break the task into atomic processes. Every motion, decision, or cycle.
Measure direct energy per cycle.
Humans: calories burned for physical work, basal metabolic rate, cognitive load estimates
Machines: kWh per operation, factoring in idle and maintenance energy
Add indirect energy per cycle.
Humans: food production, transport, tools, clothing, housing for that labor time
Machines: material embodied energy (steel, copper, silicon), fabrication, maintenance, replacement of worn parts
Sum total energy per unit output.
Divide by operational hours to get a combined “effective labor-hour” energy cost.
Task: pick and sort 1000 packages in an 8-hour shift (~125/hr)
Human baseline: walking, lifting, cognitive decisions
Average human burns ~2000 kcal/day just for basal metabolism (~8.36 MJ)
Physical labor: moderate lifting adds ~400 kcal/day (~1.67 MJ)
Total energy/day: ~10 MJ per worker
Rough estimate: ~10× direct energy → ~100 MJ/day
This accounts for embodied energy in calories, equipment, lighting, heating
Robot mass: 150 kg, motorized arms, conveyor interface
Power draw: 2 kW active, 0.5 kW idle
Task duration: 8 hours
hours active × 2 kW → 12 kWh
hours idle × 0.5 kW → 1 kWh
Total: 13 kWh (~46.8 MJ)
Lubrication, small motor wear, battery efficiency losses (~10%) → +1.3 kWh
Total energy: ~15 kWh (~54 MJ)
Suppose robot lifetime 5 years, 250 workdays/year
Robot embodied energy: ~50 GJ → 1250 shifts → 50 GJ ÷ 1250 ≈ 40 MJ per shift
MJ (operating + maintenance) + 40 MJ (embodied) = 94 MJ
Human labor required: 1 hour
Task same as above
AI relies on cloud: data updates, optimization, anomaly detection
Cloud servers located 500 km away
Cloud computation per shift: 1 TFLOP/day per robot equivalent → ~50 kWh (180 MJ)
Data transfer energy (network routers, transmission) → ~10 kWh (36 MJ)
Robot + maintenance + embedded: 54 + 40 = 94 MJ
Cloud + network: 180 + 36 = 216 MJ
Minimal human supervision: same ~1 hour
Additional: network monitoring, software patching, etc.: ~0.5 hr
Independent AI can reduce human labor drastically while slightly reducing total energy, because it doesn’t rely on distant, energy-hungry infrastructure.
Cloud AI dramatically increases total energy cost due to remote computing and data transmission, even though it further reduces labor hours slightly.
Maintenance, embedded material energy, and redistribution are non-negligible. Including them flips some naive efficiency calculations.
Humans are surprisingly competitive in energy terms, because food energy is smaller than the full lifecycle energy of cloud AI systems.
Every material (steel, copper, lithium, silicon, etc.) required for the robot
Every secondary byproduct (lubricants, wear particles, heat)
Joules required to maintain, redistribute, and replace each atom
System Definition
Task: pick and sort 1000 packages (8-hour shift)
Robot: 150 kg, articulated arms, wheels, sensors, battery
Independent: no cloud dependency
Primary materials – structural steel, motors (copper windings, magnets), sensors (silicon, silver, copper, plastics), battery (lithium, cobalt, graphite, phosphorus)
Secondary materials / byproducts – wear debris, lubricants, heat, chemical losses
Maintenance / redistribution energy – every replacement or realignment
Energy flows – electrical energy, mechanical energy, embodied energy of materials
Secondary / Waste Materials
Lubricant losses: 50 g per shift → ~1 MJ energy to produce, plus disposal energy 0.2 MJ
Wear debris: 5 g steel + 1 g copper → 0.5 MJ
Battery cycling losses: 10% energy lost as heat → 5 MJ
Heat dissipation from motors/sensors: ~10 MJ per shift
Dust / abrasion / minor plastic losses: ~0.1 MJ
Maintenance / Redistribution Energy
Setup & monitoring: 1 human-hour → ~5 MJ (including metabolic + indirect energy)
Rebalancing actuators / realignment: 2 kWh (~7.2 MJ)
Lubricant replacement: 0.2 kWh (~0.72 MJ)
Robot Operating Energy
Active motors & processors: 6 hours × 2 kW = 12 kWh (~43.2 MJ)
Idle: 2 hours × 0.5 kW = 1 kWh (~3.6 MJ)
Operating energy total: 46.8 MJ
Data center compute: 50 kWh → 180 MJ per shift
Network transmission: 10 kWh → 36 MJ
Additional maintenance for network/software monitoring: 0.5 hr human (~2.5 MJ)
Insights from Full Second-Law Accounting
Independent AI: 1 human-hour, 168 MJ per shift, mostly embedded materials amortized. Secondary waste + maintenance small but not negligible.
Cloud AI: 1.5 human-hours, 387 MJ per shift. Cloud computation dominates energy cost (~56% of total).
Entropy accounting: Every secondary material (lubricants, wear debris, heat, chemical losses) contributes to irreversible energy loss, which compounds over time.
Hidden dependencies dominate: for cloud AI, the network, data centers, and repeated software updates outweigh robot operation.
Labor hours vs energy: Reducing human labor doesn’t linearly reduce total energy or entropy cost—sometimes it increases it significantly (cloud AI).
Floor area: 2,000 m²
Steel-reinforced concrete floor, walls, ceiling
Lighting, ventilation, and conveyor systems included
Estimated 20% of material embedded energy → 22,935 MJ
Total embedded energy per robot: 114,675 + 22,935 ≈ 137,610 MJ
Total Shift Energy with Capital Infrastructure
Independent AI robot operating shift: 168.3 MJ (from previous calculation)
Warehouse per-shift amortization: 1,072 MJ
Robot amortization: 110 MJ
Cloud data center (servers, cooling, power distribution) embedded energy amortized per robot/shift: ~500 MJ
Network infrastructure (routers, cables, switching) amortized per robot/shift: ~50 MJ
Infrastructure dominates energy cost: the warehouse per shift is ~1,072 MJ, dwarfing operating energy of a single robot (~46.8 MJ).
Lifecycle energy is now >1 GJ per shift: suddenly, “energy efficient automation” is orders of magnitude more energy-intensive than a human worker (110 MJ/shift).
Cloud dependency inflates energy further: embedded cloud infrastructure now pushes energy per shift almost 2× independent AI.
Labor hours are small relative to energy: humans contribute 1–1.5 hours, but the energy required to maintain the materials and infrastructure dominates.
Hidden entropy is enormous: building, maintaining, replacing, and operating all these atoms creates vast irreversible energy dispersion—heat, worn particles, chemical losses, micro-abrasion.
Copper: 100 MJ/kg → 1.67×10⁻¹⁹ J/atom
Lithium: 5,000 MJ/kg → 8.33×10⁻¹⁸ J/atom
Cobalt: 200,000 MJ/kg → 3.33×10⁻¹⁵ J/atom
Graphite: 150 MJ/kg → 2.5×10⁻¹⁹ J/atom
Phosphate: 50 MJ/kg → 8.33×10⁻²¹ J/atom
Steel: 4.2e21 atoms × 3.33e-20 J ≈ 140 MJ
Lithium: 6e23 × 8.33e-18 J ≈ 4,998 MJ
Graphite: 9e23 × 2.5e-19 J ≈ 225 MJ
Plastics: 3e23 × 1.33e-19 J ≈ 40 MJ
Robot motors & processors: 46.8 MJ
Maintenance: 13 MJ
Warehouse amortization: 1,072 MJ
Dispersion + wear: ~505,000 MJ (dominated by cobalt & lithium)
Operating + maintenance + warehouse: 1,131.8 MJ
High-embedded-energy atoms are the real bottleneck in any automated system. Even micro-wear creates massive entropy costs.
Maintenance cycles are not the main energy cost—atom-level irreversibility is.
Cloud AI would add another ~218 MJ operating + possibly millions of MJ in server amortization per shift—still dwarfed by cobalt and lithium dispersion.
Humans remain surprisingly “efficient” per shift in energy terms, because they don’t require high-embedded-energy atoms.
Basal metabolism: ~2000 kcal/day → 8.36 MJ
Physical labor: ~400 kcal/day → 1.67 MJ
Total energy ingested: 10 MJ (matches above)
Average energy per atom used for work: 10 MJ ÷ 1.2e27 ≈ 8.3e-21 J/atom
Fraction of food atoms lost as waste, heat, or chemical inefficiency: ~60% of energy dissipated as heat → ~6 MJ lost
Fraction of atoms chemically irreversibly processed (CO₂, H₂O excretion): ~10% → 1.2e26 atoms
Atom-level irreversibility per shift (mostly lithium + cobalt wear): ~505,000 MJ
Operating + maintenance + warehouse amortization: 1,131.8 MJ
Total energy per shift: 506,131.8 MJ
Human labor hours (supervision): 1 hour
Humans are atomic-scale energy-efficient: calories are low-energy, low-embedded-energy, easily renewable.
AI is atomically “expensive”: even minimal wear in high-embedded-energy materials dominates the total energy budget.
Labor vs energy tradeoff: AI drastically reduces human labor but massively increases total irreversibility.
Cloud dependency amplifies cost: a small fraction of energy in servers and networking still adds thousands of MJ.
Second-law reality: the irreversibility of atomic-scale materials (cobalt, lithium, graphite) is the true bottleneck for automation sustainability.
For single-shift warehouse work, a human expends ~10 MJ, 8 hours labor, while an independent AI expends ~500,000 MJ, 1 hour labor.
Most energy in AI is hidden in rare high-embedded-energy atoms.
Labor reduction does not equate to energy efficiency; the thermodynamic cost is orders of magnitude higher.
